End of Unit Questions>Part I Q1

End of Unit Questions>Part I Q4

mini_headings_covered: Key Concept: A frame of reference is a set of coordinates that can be used to determine positions of objects.
Table 3.1 Difference between distance and displacement
Key Concept: Motion is the change in position
Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.

subject: PHYSICS
unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.subsection: Key Concept: Motion is the change in position
questions.structural_anchor: Key Concept: Motion is the change in position
questions.question: What is motion in physics?

options.A: Continuous change in position relative to a reference frame
options.B: The total distance traveled by an object
options.C: The speed of an object at a given instant
options.D: The force required to move an object

questions.hint: Motion requires a change in position over time relative to a fixed point.
questions.explanation: Motion is defined as the continuous change in position of an object relative to a reference frame. Option B is distance, not motion itself.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Key Concept: Motion is the change in position
source_trace.structural_element: Key Concept: Motion is the change in position
source_trace.content_anchor: Motion is a continuous change in position of an object relative to the position of a fixed object called reference frame.

explanations_tiered.definition: Motion is the continuous change in position of an object with respect to a reference frame.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: An object is in motion if its position relative to a fixed point changes over time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you walk from your desk to the door, your position changes — that's motion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Motion requires a reference frame to detect change. A body changes position relative to that frame.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In classical mechanics, motion is described by the time derivative of position: \(\vec{v} = d\vec{S}/dt\).
reasoning_steps: Identify if position changes relative to a fixed point.
If yes, the object is in motion.
misconceptions: Motion is absolute. Incorrect; motion is relative to a reference frame.
A stationary object cannot be in motion in any frame. Incorrect; it may be moving relative to another frame.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Motion = change in position relative to reference frame.

glossary_entry.definition: Continuous change in position relative to a reference frame.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Continuous change in position relative to a reference frame.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Motion is change in position relative to a reference frame.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Change in position relative to a reference frame.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Change in position relative to a reference frame.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that rest and motion are relative.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Motion requires change in position relative to a frame.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A car accelerates at \(2.5\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\) from \(7.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\) to \(12.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\). How long does it take?
questions.explanation: \(t = (12.0 - 7.0) / 2.5 = 5.0 / 2.5 = 2.0\,\mathrm{s}\). Option B would be using \(a = 2.5\) incorrectly as \(12.0/2.5 = 4.8\) not matching.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: A car travelling at 7.0 m/s accelerates at 2.5 m/s² to 12.0 m/s. How long? Δt = (12-7)/2.5 = 2 s.

explanations_tiered.definition: Time = change in velocity divided by acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearrange acceleration formula to solve for time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The car needs to increase speed by 5 m/s. At 2.5 m/s each second, it takes 2 seconds.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For constant acceleration, \(v_f = v_i + at\) ⇒ \(t = (v_f - v_i)/a\).
misconceptions: Using a = v_f/t (ignoring initial velocity).
Adding velocities instead of subtracting.

flashcard.front: a = 2.5 m/s², from 7 to 12 m/s: time?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate time from acceleration and velocity change

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: In a velocity-time graph for uniform motion, what is the slope of the line?
questions.hint: Uniform motion means constant velocity, so v does not change with time.
questions.explanation: In uniform motion, velocity is constant. On a v-t graph, a horizontal line has zero slope. Slope = acceleration = 0. Option B would be constant positive acceleration, not uniform motion.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Discuss in groups and determine the slope of the v-t graph in a uniform motion? (Zero)

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Uniform motion has constant velocity, so acceleration = 0, thus slope = 0.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the car's speed is constant, the velocity line is flat (horizontal), slope = 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Uniform motion ⇒ \(a = 0\) ⇒ slope of v-t graph = 0.
misconceptions: Slope of v-t graph in uniform motion is positive. Incorrect; only if speeding up.
Uniform motion can have non-zero slope. Incorrect; slope = acceleration = 0.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: v-t slope = a. Uniform motion ⇒ a=0 ⇒ slope=0.

glossary_entry.definition: v-t graph is horizontal line, slope = 0.

flashcard.front: v-t graph slope for uniform motion?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Uniform motion → constant v → horizontal line → slope = 0.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Interpret v-t graphs for uniform motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Uniform motion → constant v → horizontal line → slope 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a speed limit on roads?

options.A: To ensure vehicles move at instantaneous speed
options.B: To reduce the risk of accidents by controlling speed
options.C: To increase average speed of traffic
options.D: To measure the acceleration of vehicles

questions.explanation: Speed limits are set to reduce accident risk by controlling vehicle speeds. Higher speeds make accidents more severe and harder to avoid. Option A describes what a speedometer does, not the purpose of limits.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Drivers violating speed limit will be charged... vehicles moving with very high speed are main causes for death.

explanations_tiered.definition: Speed limits are regulations to control vehicle speed for safety.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Higher speeds increase stopping distance and crash severity, so limits reduce risk.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Speed limits keep cars from going too fast, so drivers have more time to stop and avoid crashes.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Speed limits reduce accident risk by limiting kinetic energy and stopping distance.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Stopping distance is proportional to \(v^2\); reducing speed dramatically decreases stopping distance and impact force.
reasoning_steps: Understand that higher speed = less control + longer stopping distance.
misconceptions: Speed limits are to annoy drivers. Incorrect; they are for safety.
Speed limits don't affect accident severity. Incorrect; higher speed increases severity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Speed limits save lives by reducing crash risk and severity.

flashcard.flashcard_back: To reduce accident risk and severity.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Speed limits reduce accident risk by controlling speed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss real-world application of speed concepts.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Speed limits are for safety, not just enforcement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A driver believes that driving just below the speed limit eliminates all risk of accidents. Why is this belief incorrect?

options.A: Because other factors like weather and road conditions also affect safety
options.B: Because speed limits are only suggestions
options.C: Because slower speeds always cause more accidents
options.D: Because speed limits apply only to highways

questions.hint: Safety depends on more than just speed.
questions.explanation: While speed limits reduce risk, other factors such as weather, road conditions, visibility, and driver attention are also critical. Option A correctly identifies that speed alone does not guarantee safety.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Discuss in groups and identify the type of vehicles causing accidents... percentage of accidents caused by violation of speed limits.

explanations_tiered.definition: Accident risk depends on multiple factors beyond speed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Safe driving requires appropriate speed for conditions, not just staying under the limit.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Even if you drive slowly, rain, ice, or a distracted driver can still cause a crash.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Stopping distance depends on speed, but also on tire condition, road surface, and reaction time.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The total stopping distance \(d = v t_r + v^2/(2\mu g)\) includes reaction time \(t_r\) and friction \(\mu\), both affected by conditions.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that multiple factors affect safety.
Understand that speed limits are a baseline, not absolute guarantee.
misconceptions: Following the speed limit makes you completely safe. Incorrect; conditions matter.
Only speed affects accident severity. Incorrect; vehicle mass, road conditions, etc. also matter.

flashcard.front: Does driving at speed limit guarantee safety?
flashcard.flashcard_back: No; conditions like weather and road also matter.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Other factors (weather, road, attention) also affect risk.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is speed limit enough for safety?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No; conditions like weather and road also matter.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Other factors (weather, road, attention) also affect risk.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What type of quantity is displacement?
questions.explanation: Displacement has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity. Distance is scalar. Option B is distance, not displacement.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Displacement is the change in position of a body in a certain direction.

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors require both size and direction for complete description.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Displacement tells you not just how far, but which way — like 5 km north.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Displacement \(\Delta \vec{S}\) includes direction, making it a vector. Distance is scalar.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vectors obey vector algebra; displacement adds via parallelogram law.
misconceptions: Displacement is scalar. Incorrect; it has direction.
All motion quantities are vectors. Incorrect; distance and speed are scalars.
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Displacement includes direction → vector.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Displacement includes direction → vector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: In uniform motion, how are average velocity and instantaneous velocity related?
questions.explanation: In uniform motion, velocity is constant. Therefore, instantaneous velocity at any instant equals the constant velocity, which also equals the average velocity over any interval. Option A is correct.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: How are average velocity and instantaneous velocity related in a uniform motion? (They are the same)

explanations_tiered.definition: In uniform motion, velocity is constant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Constant velocity means instantaneous velocity = constant = average velocity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drive at a steady 60 km/h, your speed at any moment is 60 km/h, and your average over any time is also 60 km/h.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Uniform motion ⇒ \(v(t) = v_0\) constant. Then \(v_{avg} = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \int v dt = v_0 = v_{inst}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For constant velocity, the velocity function is constant; its average over any interval equals that constant.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of uniform motion (constant velocity).
Average velocity over any interval also = constant.
misconceptions: They are never equal. Incorrect; they are equal in uniform motion.
They are only equal if acceleration is non-zero. Incorrect; equal when acceleration=0.

flashcard.front: In uniform motion, v_avg vs v_inst?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Uniform motion → constant v → v_avg = v_inst.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate average and instantaneous velocity in uniform motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Constant velocity → v_avg = v_inst.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: In a uniform motion, what does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
questions.hint: For any motion, area under v-t = displacement.
questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph equals the displacement. In uniform motion, it's a rectangle: area = v × t = displacement. Option A is slope, not area.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: What does the area under velocity against time graph describe in a uniform motion? (displacement)

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For uniform motion, v constant, area = v × Δt = displacement.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you go 10 m/s for 5 s, the rectangle area (10×5=50) is the distance you covered.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Area under v-t = ∫ v dt = displacement. For constant v, area = v Δt = ΔS.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement \(\Delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) dt\). For uniform motion, \(v(t)=v_0\), so \(\Delta S = v_0 \Delta t\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that area under v-t = displacement.
Uniform motion gives a rectangle, area = v × t.
misconceptions: Area under v-t = acceleration. Incorrect; slope = acceleration.
Area under v-t = distance only if direction constant? Actually area = displacement (signed).

flashcard.front: What does area under v-t graph represent?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Area under v-t = displacement.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: If the slope of a distance-time graph is zero, what can be concluded about the motion?

options.B: The object is moving with constant speed

questions.hint: Slope of s-t graph = velocity. Zero slope = zero velocity.
questions.explanation: Slope of a distance-time (position-time) graph equals velocity. Zero slope means velocity = 0, so the object is at rest. Option B would be a non-zero constant slope.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: If the slope of the graph is zero in a distance against time graph, what can one conclude about the motion of the body? (object at rest)

explanations_tiered.definition: Slope of s-t graph = velocity. Zero slope means v=0.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If position does not change with time, the object is at rest.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A flat line on a distance-time graph means you're not moving — you're at rest.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Slope = ΔS/Δt = velocity. Zero slope ⇒ ΔS=0 ⇒ no change in position ⇒ rest.
explanations_tiered.advanced: v = dS/dt = 0 ⇒ S(t) = constant ⇒ object stationary.
misconceptions: Zero slope means constant speed. Incorrect; constant speed requires non-zero slope.
Zero slope means negative acceleration. Incorrect; no motion.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Zero slope on s-t = at rest.

glossary_entry.definition: Zero velocity, no change in position.

flashcard.front: Zero slope on distance-time graph means?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Slope = velocity; zero slope = zero velocity = rest.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Zero slope on s-t graph means?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Slope = velocity; zero slope = rest.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: When is the acceleration of a body said to be positive?

options.A: When its velocity increases in the positive direction

questions.hint: Positive acceleration means velocity becomes more positive (or less negative).
questions.explanation: Positive acceleration occurs when the change in velocity (v_f − v_i) is in the positive direction. For motion along a line, this typically means speeding up in the positive direction. Option B describes negative acceleration if direction is positive.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: When do we say that the acceleration of a body is (a) positive? (b) negative?

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration is positive when velocity increases in the positive direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Positive acceleration means Δv is positive (v_f > v_i for positive direction).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're driving forward and press the gas, you speed up — that's positive acceleration.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Positive acceleration occurs when \(a = (v_f - v_i)/\Delta t > 0\), meaning velocity becomes more positive.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Acceleration \(\vec{a} = d\vec{v}/dt\) is positive if the velocity vector increases in the chosen positive direction.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of acceleration as change in velocity over time.
Positive if Δv is in positive direction.
Often means speeding up in positive direction.
misconceptions: Positive acceleration always means speeding up. Incorrect; if velocity is negative, positive acceleration means slowing down in the negative direction.
Acceleration sign is independent of velocity direction. Incorrect; they are related by Δv.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Positive a = velocity increases in positive direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Change in velocity is in the positive direction.

flashcard.flashcard_back: When velocity increases in the positive direction.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Positive acceleration = velocity increases in positive direction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: When velocity increases in the positive direction.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Positive a = velocity increases in positive direction.
